proposals.title: Reconceptualizing the Teacher-in-Action: A Grounded Theory Study of Embedded Professional Learning in Music Education
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 music teacher professional development (PD) is often critiqued as being decontextualized, episodic, and separate from daily practice, limiting its impact. This research addresses the persistent theory-practice gap by challenging the foundational assumption that PD is an activity distinct from teaching. It matters because understanding teaching as a site of continuous, embedded learning could fundamentally reshape how we support teacher growth and effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that music teachers engage in significant, deliberate professional learning *during* the act of teaching itself, and that these embedded learning processes are characterized by distinct, identifiable cognitive and reflective patterns. This challenges the notion that PD must be a separate, formalized event.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a constructivist grounded theory methodology. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 20-25 practicing K-12 music teachers, focusing on critical incidents where they felt they learned something significant *while* teaching. Concurrently, we will collect and analyze teacher-generated artifacts (e.g., lesson plans with in-the-moment annotations, post-teaching audio memos). Data analysis will follow the constant comparative method using NVivo software to code for themes and develop a theoretical model of in-action teacher learning.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a new theoretical framework for understanding teacher learning as an embedded, continuous process. It will provide concrete evidence and language to advocate for PD models that leverage and support in-the-moment learning, moving beyond the traditional 'PD as separate event' paradigm. The findings will directly inform the design of more responsive and contextualized teacher education and support systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques traditional PD, this study uniquely shifts the focus to empirically investigating the teaching act *itself* as a primary locus of professional development. It moves beyond advocating for 'job-embedded' PD to theorizing the intrinsic learning mechanisms of teaching practice.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing Support for Veteran Music Teachers: A Study of Differentiated Mentoring and Self-Directed Professional Learning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, music teacher professional development and mentoring literature is heavily weighted toward induction and early-career support, implicitly framing experienced teachers as stable or self-sufficient. This creates a gap where the complex, evolving needs of veteran teachers—such as combating burnout, navigating curricular reforms, or integrating new technologies—are underserved by one-size-fits-all PD models. Addressing this gap is crucial for sustaining a high-quality, adaptable music teaching workforce throughout the career continuum.
proposals.hypothesis: Experienced music teachers (10+ years) will demonstrate greater professional growth and satisfaction when provided with flexible, self-directed support structures (e.g., peer coaching networks, inquiry projects) compared to traditional, structured mentoring programs designed for novices. Their needs are more effectively met by frameworks that leverage their expertise and autonomy.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will employ a comparative case study design. Approximately 40 experienced music teachers will be purposively sampled and assigned to two conditions: one participating in a structured, formal mentoring program (control) and the other in a self-directed, resource-facilitated model (treatment). Data collection will include pre/post surveys measuring professional efficacy and engagement, analysis of reflective journals and peer-coaching logs, and semi-structured interviews. Qualitative data will be analyzed using thematic analysis, and quantitative data will be analyzed using comparative statistics to identify significant differences in outcomes between groups.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced model for differentiated professional learning that recognizes career-stage-specific needs, moving beyond the deficit-based support model for beginners. It will offer concrete strategies for districts and teacher educators to design cost-effective, sustainable support systems that retain and rejuvenate veteran music educators, thereby enriching the entire school music program.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the predominant assumption in music teacher education that formal, structured support is primarily for novices, instead positing that experienced teachers require fundamentally different, less structured forms of support to thrive. It shifts the focus from remediation to leveraging expertise for continuous renewal.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Pinnacle: A Phenomenological Study of Music Teacher Professional Development Beyond Graduate Education
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 discourse in music teacher education often positions graduate degrees as the ultimate, culminating form of professional development (PD), potentially devaluing other learning pathways. This hierarchical view can create barriers for practicing teachers and fails to account for the complex, situated, and ongoing nature of professional growth. Grounded in Conway's prior work critiquing traditional PD, this study addresses a critical gap in understanding how experienced music teachers conceptualize and engage in meaningful, self-directed professional learning after or instead of formal graduate study.
proposals.hypothesis: Experienced in-service music teachers who have chosen not to pursue or have completed graduate degrees will identify and engage in rich, self-sustaining ecosystems of professional development that they perceive as more relevant, responsive, and impactful than formal graduate coursework. These ecosystems are characterized by peer collaboration, targeted skill acquisition, and direct classroom application.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a phenomenological design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 15-20 in-service music teachers (5+ years experience) across K-12 settings, purposively sampled to include those with no graduate degrees, those with terminal degrees, and those with degrees but who report more significant learning elsewhere. Interview transcripts will be analyzed using Moustakas's modification of the Stevick-Colaizzi-Keen method, involving horizontalization, thematic clustering, and the development of textural and structural descriptions to distill the essence of participants' lived experiences with PD beyond graduate school.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, teacher-centered model of professional learning that challenges the presumed superiority of formal graduate education. It will offer concrete evidence for reconceptualizing PD in music teacher education literature and policy, advocating for systems that validate and integrate diverse, teacher-led learning pathways. The findings will directly inform the design of alternative PD structures and graduate program curricula to better serve practicing educators.
proposals.novelty: While Conway's prior work critiques traditional PD formats, this study uniquely targets the specific, often-unquestioned status of graduate school as the PD ideal. It shifts the focus from evaluating existing PD structures to exploring the organic, self-generated learning systems teachers build when formal graduate education is not perceived as the optimal solution.

proposals.title: Validating a Micro-Credentialing Framework for Incremental, Competency-Based Music Teacher Development
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research critiques traditional professional development (PD) for music teachers as episodic, disconnected from practice, and lacking recognition for incremental growth. Concurrently, digital badging and micro-credentials, adapted from corporate and technology training, show promise in other educational fields for motivating learners and documenting granular skill acquisition. Applying this model to music teacher education could address persistent gaps in personalized, ongoing teacher learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A micro-credentialing system, co-designed with in-service music teachers, will be perceived as more relevant, motivating, and supportive of incremental skill development than traditional workshop-based PD models. We hypothesize that teacher engagement and perceived competency gains will be higher for micro-credential activities.
proposals.method: This design-based research study will involve three iterative cycles over 18 months. First, we will conduct focus groups with K-12 music teachers to co-design a framework of 15-20 micro-credentials aligned with specific pedagogical competencies (e.g., 'Facilitating Student-Led Composition in Ensemble Settings'). Second, we will develop and pilot the digital badges and associated competency-based assessments within a professional learning network. Third, we will collect mixed-methods data including teacher surveys (measuring motivation, perceived relevance), analysis of badge-earning patterns, and semi-structured interviews to evaluate the system's impact on teacher practice and its usability.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a validated, practitioner-informed micro-credentialing framework specifically for music teacher development, a novel contribution to the field. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of competency-based, digital recognition systems for supporting sustained professional growth, moving beyond theoretical discussion to practical implementation and evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While micro-credentials are discussed in general education, their application to the specific, complex skill set of music teaching—integrating performance, pedagogy, and technology—is unexplored. The novelty lies in adapting and rigorously testing this corporate-derived model within the aesthetic and practical context of music education, directly addressing calls to 'rethink' music teacher learning.

proposals.title: Adapting Japanese Lesson Study for Collaborative, Classroom-Based Professional Development in Music Education: A Design-Based Research Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Conway's own work, critiques traditional, top-down professional development (PD) for music teachers as often fragmented and disconnected from classroom practice. There is a recognized need for sustained, collaborative, and practice-based models that foster deep pedagogical learning. The Japanese lesson study model, proven effective in general education for improving instruction through collaborative planning, observation, and reflection, offers a promising but under-explored framework for music-specific PD.
proposals.hypothesis: That a culturally and pedagogically adapted version of the Japanese lesson study model will be a feasible and effective mechanism for fostering collaborative, reflective practice among in-service music teachers, leading to perceived improvements in their pedagogical knowledge and instructional strategies.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-phase design-based research (DBR) approach. Phase 1 involves a systematic review of lesson study adaptations in other subject areas and focus groups with music educators to design a prototype music lesson study (MLS) framework. Phase 2 implements the MLS framework in two cycles with a pilot group of 6-8 in-service music teachers. Data collection will include video recordings of study group meetings and research lessons, pre/post interviews, teacher reflections, and field notes. Analysis will use qualitative thematic coding to assess feasibility, participant engagement, and perceived impact on practice.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a concrete, adaptable model for job-embedded, collaborative PD specifically designed for the context of music teaching. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and initial outcomes of lesson study in music education, addressing gaps in the literature on effective music teacher learning. The resulting framework and case studies will serve as a practical guide for music teacher educators and district leaders seeking to move beyond traditional workshop-based PD.
proposals.novelty: While lesson study has been adapted in STEM and literacy fields, its systematic application and study within the unique, often performance-based and creative context of music education is novel. This project moves beyond simply importing the model to actively redesign it through DBR to address the specific pedagogical content knowledge and instructional challenges of music teachers.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing Professional Growth: A Student-Centered, Evidence-Based Model for In-Service Music Teacher Development
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in music teacher education critiques traditional, workshop-based professional development (PD) as often disconnected from classroom realities and teacher needs. Concurrently, educational policy increasingly emphasizes student outcomes as the ultimate measure of teaching effectiveness. This proposal addresses the gap by directly linking teacher learning to its most meaningful consequence: demonstrable student growth, thereby creating a more authentic and sustainable PD model.
proposals.hypothesis: When in-service music teachers engage in a structured cycle of planning, implementing, and analyzing evidence of student learning (e.g., skill acquisition, engagement, creativity), their own pedagogical content knowledge and reflective practice will show greater and more enduring growth compared to teachers participating in content-delivery-focused PD workshops.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods design-based research study will involve 15 in-service music teachers over one academic year. Teachers will co-design instructional units with researchers, then implement them in their classrooms. Primary data on teacher development will be derived inversely from systematic, multi-modal evidence of student learning: (1) pre/post audio-video assessments of student musical performance analyzed with acoustic feature extraction (e.g., timing, intonation accuracy via Tony), (2) student engagement metrics from wearable sensors (e.g., E4 wristband) during ensemble rehearsals, and (3) student reflective journals. Teacher growth will be measured through qualitative analysis of their collaborative video analysis sessions of this student data and pre/post interviews, tracking shifts in pedagogical reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, evidence-based framework for student-centered music teacher PD, moving the field beyond satisfaction surveys. It will contribute a novel methodology for treating student learning artifacts as primary data for teacher development research and offer practical tools for districts to evaluate PD impact more meaningfully.
proposals.novelty: While teacher reflection on student work is established, this study innovates by formally inverting the PD evaluation model, making aggregated, multi-faceted student learning the direct input and catalyst for teacher learning, rather than a distal outcome. It integrates contemporary biometric and computational music analysis tools specifically for this inverse assessment purpose in music education.

proposals.title: Bridging the Apprenticeship Gap: A Design-Based Study of Co-Constructed Professional Development for Preservice and Inservice Music Teachers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature in music teacher education critiques traditional professional development (PD) for inservice teachers as often fragmented and disconnected from practice, while preservice teachers' learning is frequently siloed in university settings. This creates a persistent apprenticeship gap where novice and experienced teachers rarely engage in collaborative, ongoing learning. Addressing this gap is crucial for fostering a more cohesive, continuous, and practice-based professional learning continuum.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-designed PD experiences that jointly engage preservice student teachers and their inservice mentor teachers will lead to (1) enhanced pedagogical content knowledge for both groups, and (2) the development of more robust, reciprocal communities of practice that challenge traditional hierarchical models of teacher learning.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, multi-case design-based research (DBR) methodology. Over three iterative cycles, we will co-design and implement three distinct PD modules (e.g., on culturally responsive pedagogy, technology integration, and assessment) with 6-8 preservice-inservice dyads. Data collection will include pre/post semi-structured interviews, video recordings of collaborative planning and PD sessions, reflective journals, and field notes. Analysis will use a constant comparative approach to code for themes of knowledge construction, role negotiation, and community development.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete, evidence-based model for integrated teacher learning that bridges the preservice-inservice divide. It will provide the field with practical frameworks for designing collaborative PD and new theoretical insights into how reciprocal mentoring and co-construction of knowledge function within music education.
proposals.novelty: While mentoring is common, the novelty lies in systematically designing PD *for and with* both groups as equal learners, moving beyond the mentor-as-supervisor model to a co-constructive partnership focused on mutual professional growth.

proposals.title: Designing Proactive Pipelines: A Systems-Level Intervention for Demographic Parity in Music Teacher Education Faculty
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, music teacher education faculty demographics often lag behind broader higher education faculty averages, particularly in racial and ethnic diversity. This lack of representation limits the role models and pedagogical perspectives available to pre-service music teachers, who will serve increasingly diverse student populations. Addressing this requires moving beyond reactive hiring to proactive, systemic pipeline development.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted, multi-institutional intervention program for graduate students and early-career professionals from underrepresented groups, coupled with structured mentorship and professional socialization, will produce a candidate pool for music teacher education faculty positions that exceeds the demographic parity of general higher education faculty within a five-year cohort cycle.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods design-based research study will first establish a 2025 baseline via a national survey of music teacher education faculty demographics and an analysis of job advertisements and hires from 2020-2025. The core intervention is the co-design and implementation of a 'Future Faculty Fellowship' program across three partner universities. The program will include funded doctoral support, structured mentorship networks with existing faculty, and guided professional development (e.g., publishing, grant writing). Data collection will involve tracking participant career trajectories, pre/post-program surveys on self-efficacy and professional identity, and qualitative interviews with fellows and mentors to refine the pipeline model iteratively.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a tested, actionable model for building a more diverse music teacher education professoriate, directly addressing calls for equity in the field. It will contribute empirical evidence on the efficacy of specific pipeline strategies and generate a framework for longitudinal, collaborative faculty development that moves beyond isolated professional development events.
proposals.novelty: While diversity in music education is a recognized concern, this study uniquely applies a proactive, systems-level 'pipeline design' approach—common in STEM fields—to music teacher education, focusing on structural intervention rather than merely documenting disparity or advising on inclusive hiring practices post-vacancy.

proposals.title: Backward Design for Music Teacher Preparation: A Mixed-Methods Study to Derive Programmatic Features from Desired Educator Traits
proposals.motivation: Traditional music teacher education often follows a forward-design model, sequencing courses and experiences without explicitly linking them to a validated, holistic profile of an effective early-career educator. This has led to critiques of professional development being disconnected from classroom realities. A systematic, inverse-design approach is needed to ensure preparation programs are coherently engineered to produce teachers with specific, research-based competencies and dispositions.
proposals.hypothesis: By first establishing a consensus-based profile of desired traits for early-career music educators, it is possible to identify and prioritize specific, actionable features of teacher preparation programs (e.g., course sequencing, field experience structures, mentoring models) that are most predictive of developing those traits.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will employ a sequential exploratory design. Phase 1: Conduct Delphi studies with panels of exemplary in-service teachers, teacher educators, and school administrators to establish a ranked consensus on critical early-career educator traits. Phase 2: Use these traits as output variables in a series of retrospective surveys and program document analyses with recent graduates (N=200+) from diverse programs. Phase 3: Apply Classification and Regression Tree (CART) analysis and multiple regression to identify which self-reported program experiences and structural features are most strongly associated with the development of the highest-priority traits.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a data-driven framework for the backward design of music teacher education curricula, moving beyond anecdote or tradition. It will produce a transferable methodology for program evaluation and a set of evidence-based design principles that can be adapted by music teacher educators to improve program coherence and effectiveness.
proposals.novelty: While backward design (Wiggins & McTighe) is established in curriculum theory, its rigorous, empirical application to derive structural program features from a trait-based outcomes model is novel in music teacher education. This study innovates by applying advanced statistical techniques (CART) typically used in educational data mining to solve the 'inverse problem' of program design.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing the Cooperating Teacher Role: A Design-Based Study of a Student-Teacher-Centric Placement Model in Music Education
proposals.motivation: Traditional student teaching placements often position the cooperating teacher (CT) as a classroom manager first and a mentor second, implicitly prioritizing P-12 student learning over the development of the novice. This creates a tension that can limit the student teacher's opportunities for meaningful, scaffolded practice and reflective risk-taking. Grounded in pre-2026 critiques of traditional professional development, this study addresses a core structural problem in music teacher preparation.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, student-teacher-centric placement model, where the CT's primary defined goal is the developmental progression of the student teacher, will lead to significant, measurable gains in the student teacher's self-efficacy and pedagogical skill, without detriment to P-12 student engagement or achievement.
proposals.method: This study employs a multi-phase, design-based research methodology within instrumental music settings. We will co-design and iteratively test a 'Developmental Placement Model' (DPM) with a cohort of CTs and student teachers. The DPM includes: 1) a structured co-planning protocol that explicitly allocates increasing teaching responsibility, 2) mandatory, framed post-teaching debriefs using video-stimulated recall, and 3) weekly reflective journals from both CT and student teacher focused on novice growth. Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis of qualitative data and pre/post quantitative measures of student teacher self-efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a tested, practical framework for redefining the CT role, moving beyond the 'prioritize P-12 learning' dilemma. It contributes directly to the field of music teacher education by offering a model that aligns structural practice with the espoused goal of teacher development, a key concern in pre-2026 literature on inservice teacher learning.
proposals.novelty: While mentoring is well-studied, this proposal explicitly inverts the traditional priority of the placement, making student teacher development the contractually primary objective, and provides a concrete, operational model to test this paradigm shift—a direct empirical test of a principle often assumed but not structurally enforced.

proposals.title: Resistance and Role Strain: A Phenomenological Study of Cooperating Music Teachers' Experiences with Multifaceted Mentoring Expectations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature in music teacher education often idealizes the cooperating teacher (CT) role, assuming CTs readily embrace complex duties as modelers, coaches, gatekeepers, and co-learners. This uncritical assumption overlooks the significant emotional labor and practical burdens of mentoring, potentially leading to CT burnout and ineffective student teacher placements. Understanding the experiences of CTs who feel resistance or strain is crucial for designing sustainable, supportive partnership models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of cooperating music teachers experience role conflict and burden due to multifaceted mentoring expectations, and that these experiences are shaped more by systemic constraints (e.g., lack of time, compensation, training) and personal identity than by a simple lack of willingness.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a phenomenological design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 15-20 practicing music teachers who have served as CTs within the last three years. Interview protocols will explore perceptions of role expectations, sources of burden or conflict, and emotional responses. Data analysis will follow Moustakas's transcendental phenomenological approach, involving horizontalization, thematic clustering, and textural-structural synthesis to develop composite descriptions of the essence of the experience.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, evidence-based counter-narrative to idealized CT roles, directly informing the design of more realistic CT recruitment, preparation, and support programs. It will contribute to the 'rethinking' of music teacher learning called for in recent scholarship by centering the CT's lived experience as a critical site for professional development reform.
proposals.novelty: While prior research documents CT roles and challenges, this study deliberately investigates the under-examined phenomena of resistance and burden, moving beyond a deficit framework to understand these responses as rational reactions to systemic conditions. It applies a phenomenological lens specifically to the negative or conflicted dimensions of the CT experience in music education.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing the Student Teaching Triad: A Participatory Action Research Study on Cooperating Teacher-Led Curriculum and Assessment
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature in music teacher education critiques traditional professional development and university-led student teaching models as often hierarchical and misaligned with the practical realities of K-12 classrooms. This perpetuates a theory-practice divide and can marginalize the expertise of cooperating teachers (CTs), who are essential for novice teacher development. Inverting the power dynamic in university-CT partnerships directly addresses calls to rethink inservice teacher learning and create more collaborative, practice-centered models.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured program where cooperating teachers co-design and lead the core curriculum and assessment for their student teachers will: 1) increase CTs' sense of professional agency and leadership, and 2) result in student teacher learning experiences perceived as more relevant and authentically embedded in the school context.
proposals.method: This study employs a participatory action research (PAR) design across three university music education programs. Over one academic year, a cohort of 12 experienced CTs will form a design team with 3 university faculty facilitators. Using iterative design cycles (Plan-Act-Observe-Reflect), the CT team will develop the core learning objectives, seminar topics, and competency-based assessment rubrics for their student teachers. Data collection will include pre/post interviews with CTs and student teachers, reflective journals, recordings of design team meetings, and analysis of the co-created curricular artifacts. Thematic analysis will be used to identify patterns in perceptions of agency, relevance, and partnership dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a concrete, empirically tested model for decentralizing authority in teacher preparation, positioning CTs as true co-teacher educators. It will contribute new knowledge on the impact of such models on CT professional growth and student teacher preparedness. The findings will offer a practical framework for programs seeking to move beyond traditional, university-centric partnership structures.
proposals.novelty: While collaboration is a known ideal, existing models typically keep curriculum and high-stakes assessment firmly under university control. This proposal's novelty lies in systematically transferring primary authority for these core components to the CT community, fundamentally restructuring the partnership's power architecture and testing its outcomes.

proposals.title: Navigating Dual Mandates: A Qualitative Study of Cooperating Music Teachers' Strategies for Balancing P-12 Student Learning and Student Teacher Mentorship
proposals.motivation: In music teacher education, the cooperating teacher (CT) is a critical linchpin, tasked with the dual—and often competing—mandate of ensuring high-quality learning for their P-12 students while providing effective mentorship to a student teacher. Pre-2026 literature in music education and teacher preparation frequently acknowledges this tension but lacks detailed, empirical studies on the specific, in-situ strategies CTs employ to manage it. This gap limits the development of targeted supports for these educators, who are essential for the professional development of future teachers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that experienced cooperating music teachers employ a repertoire of deliberate, context-sensitive pedagogical and relational strategies that allow them to dynamically prioritize and integrate the learning needs of both P-12 students and their student teachers, rather than viewing these goals as a zero-sum trade-off.
proposals.method: This study will employ a qualitative, multiple-case study design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 12-15 purposefully selected cooperating music teachers from diverse school settings (e.g., elementary, secondary, urban, suburban). To ground the interviews in practice, we will also collect and analyze lesson plans, reflective journals, and video recordings of teaching episodes (taught by both the CT and student teacher) using thematic analysis. The analysis will follow an iterative, constant-comparative approach to identify, categorize, and theorize the specific strategies used to navigate the dual learning environment.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, practice-based framework of strategies used by cooperating music teachers, moving beyond abstract principles to actionable knowledge. It will contribute directly to the improvement of music teacher education by informing more effective CT training, mentorship models, and university partnership protocols, ultimately strengthening the clinical preparation of future music educators.
proposals.novelty: While the tension is acknowledged, this study uniquely focuses on the *specific, observable strategies* of balance as its primary unit of analysis, moving past general descriptions of challenge to document the actionable 'how' of successful mentorship in the live classroom context.

proposals.title: The Evolving Mentor: A Longitudinal, Multi-Case Study of Cooperating Teacher Perceptions Across Sequential Student Teacher Placements
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in music teacher education has critiqued traditional professional development models and emphasized the critical role of cooperating teachers (CTs) as mentors. However, studies of CT development are typically cross-sectional, capturing a single moment in time. This limits understanding of how mentoring philosophies and practices are shaped by cumulative experience across multiple student teacher partnerships, a significant gap in preparing effective clinical educators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cooperating teachers' perceptions of their role, mentoring strategies, and the goals of student teaching will evolve in non-linear ways across sequential placements, moving from a focus on technical skill replication toward a more nuanced, dialogic, and adaptive model of co-learning.
proposals.method: This qualitative longitudinal study will employ a collective case study design, tracking 8-10 experienced music CTs over 2-3 consecutive academic years (spanning 4-6 student teacher placements). Data collection will involve semi-structured interviews at the start, midpoint, and end of each placement, alongside reflective journals and analysis of CT-student teacher communication (e.g., email logs). Data will be analyzed using a multi-phase thematic analysis, first within each CT's longitudinal timeline to identify personal trajectories, followed by a cross-case analysis using constant comparative methods to identify overarching patterns of perceptual change.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of CT perceptual development, directly informing the design of more effective, sustained support systems for cooperating teachers beyond one-off training workshops. It will contribute a new theoretical framework for understanding mentor teacher learning as an iterative, experience-based process, offering concrete insights for music teacher educators and program coordinators on how to structure partnerships and facilitate CT growth.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines CT perceptions or student teacher experience in isolation, this study's longitudinal, multi-placement design is novel, allowing it to trace the *process* of mentor development influenced by repeated cycles of practice and reflection with different novices.

proposals.title: Mapping the In-the-Moment Mentoring Decisions of Cooperating Music Teachers: A Cognitive Task Analysis
proposals.motivation: Cooperating Teachers (CTs) are critical for preservice music teacher development, yet their real-time, in-the-moment mentoring decisions are poorly understood. Traditional professional development often fails to address this complex, situated expertise. Grounded in pre-2026 calls to rethink music teacher learning, this study aims to make the implicit, expert judgments of CTs explicit to improve mentoring practice and preservice teacher education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that expert music teacher mentors utilize a structured, though often tacit, cognitive framework when making in-the-moment instructional decisions during a student teacher's lesson. This framework can be systematically mapped and will reveal key decision points and knowledge cues distinct from novice mentors.
proposals.method: This study will employ a qualitative Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA) methodology, specifically the Critical Decision Method. We will recruit 10-12 expert cooperating music teachers. Data collection will involve video-stimulated recall interviews, where CTs review recordings of their student teachers' lessons and verbalize their moment-by-moment observations, judgments, and potential interventions. Interview transcripts will be analyzed using established CTA coding techniques to identify decision requirements, critical cues, situational assessments, and goal hierarchies that constitute their mentoring expertise.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a descriptive model of music teacher mentoring cognition, directly informing the design of more effective, practice-based professional learning for CTs. It will contribute a novel, expertise-focused perspective to the music teacher education literature, moving beyond generic mentoring frameworks to domain-specific decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While CTA is established in other teaching domains, its application to map the specific in-the-moment cognitive processes of cooperating *music* teachers is novel. This approach moves beyond surveying CT perceptions or outcomes to directly analyze the cognitive architecture of expert mentoring judgment in a music-specific context.

proposals.title: Navigating the Critical Incident: A Micro-Analysis of Mentor-Student Teacher Tension in Music Education
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in music teacher education acknowledges that the cooperating teacher (CT)-student teacher relationship is pivotal for professional development, yet often treats it as a monolithic experience. Traditional professional development models fail to address the complex, situated, and often emotionally charged moments where learning and conflict intersect. A deep, contextualized understanding of these critical incidents is essential to move beyond generic mentoring frameworks and develop more responsive, effective support for both parties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, challenging mentoring incident, when subjected to micro-analysis, will reveal a complex interplay of pedagogical beliefs, identity negotiation, and unspoken relational dynamics that standard evaluation tools and post-hoc interviews typically miss.
proposals.method: This qualitative case study will employ Interaction Analysis (IA) of a single video-recorded critical incident (e.g., a post-lesson debrief conflict). The analysis will involve a multi-phase process: 1) Transcribing verbal and salient non-verbal communication. 2) Iterative, line-by-line coding for discursive moves (e.g., questioning, justifying, redirecting), affective tone, and power dynamics. 3) Using constant comparative analysis to build themes from the coded data, informed by frameworks of situated learning and teacher identity. Member checking with the CT and student teacher will be used to validate interpretations.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a rich, nuanced exemplar of mentor-student teacher interaction at a moment of tension, providing the field with a methodological model for micro-analysis of practice. It will generate actionable knowledge about the real-time mechanics of mentoring challenges, informing the design of more targeted coaching resources and reflective protocols for music teacher educators and professional development facilitators.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies the importance of mentoring, this study's novelty lies in its radical focus on a single incident and its use of fine-grained interaction analysis to unpack the *process* of relational and pedagogical negotiation, rather than just reporting outcomes or perceptions.

proposals.title: From Feedback to Framework: A Qualitative Case Study of University Music Teacher Education Program Adaptation in Response to Cooperating Teacher Input
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in music teacher education consistently highlights the critical role of cooperating teachers (CTs) in student teacher development, yet the flow of feedback is largely unidirectional. While CTs mentor students, there is limited systematic understanding of how university programs receive, interpret, and institutionalize CT feedback to improve curriculum and clinical experiences. This gap represents a missed opportunity for authentic, practice-informed program evolution, a key concern in Conway's prior work on rethinking professional learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that university music teacher education programs engage in a multi-stage, socially negotiated process of sensemaking when receiving CT feedback, leading to adaptations that are often incremental and contextual rather than transformative, due to institutional constraints and divergent epistemic frameworks between universities and schools.
proposals.method: This qualitative instrumental case study will employ document analysis, semi-structured interviews, and focus groups. We will purposively sample one university music education program and its network of CTs. Data collection will include: 1) analysis of formal CT feedback forms and program revision documents; 2) iterative interviews with program faculty, coordinators, and administrators; and 3) focus groups with CTs. Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis, specifically employing a multi-cycle coding process (descriptive, pattern, and thematic coding) to construct a model of the feedback assimilation process.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a grounded model of how music teacher education programs process external feedback, moving beyond the student-teacher-CT triad to examine the programmatic level. It will provide concrete recommendations for structuring more reciprocal, systemic feedback loops between universities and school partners, directly addressing calls for clinically rich teacher preparation.
proposals.novelty: While CT feedback on individual student teachers is well-studied, this project novelly inverts the lens to investigate the university as the learner and adaptive system, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 music teacher education literature. It connects Conway's critique of traditional professional development to the ongoing development of the teacher education program itself.

proposals.title: Navigating the Apprenticeship of Observation: A Longitudinal, Multi-Method Study of Early Field Experiences on Preservice Music Teachers' Beliefs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in music teacher education acknowledges that preservice teachers enter programs with deeply ingrained beliefs from their own 'apprenticeship of observation' as students, which can conflict with contemporary pedagogical frameworks. However, the specific mechanisms through which early, structured field experiences (like practicum and observation) interact with and reshape these initial beliefs remain underexplored, creating a gap in designing effective, belief-conscious teacher preparation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early, scaffolded field experiences will not uniformly shift preservice music teachers' beliefs toward program-aligned, research-based practices, but will instead create identifiable, belief-based 'profiles' (e.g., reinforced, conflicted, transformed) influenced by the congruence between prior beliefs and mentor teacher practices.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods longitudinal study will track two cohorts of preservice music teachers (N=40) across their first two field experiences. Data collection will include: (1) Pre/post belief surveys using adapted items from the 'Music Teachers' Beliefs Questionnaire'; (2) Semi-structured interviews and guided journaling at three time points, analyzed via thematic analysis; and (3) Systematic observation of mentor teachers using a modified version of the 'Rehearsal Frame Analysis' protocol to code for instructional focus. Data integration will use a convergent parallel design, with quantitative belief change scores correlated with qualitative themes and observational data to construct belief change narratives.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, process-oriented model of belief development in early field experiences, moving beyond simple 'change/no change' metrics. It will offer concrete, evidence-based strategies for music teacher educators to design field placements and reflective activities that proactively engage with and challenge preservice teachers' prior beliefs, thereby strengthening the link between university coursework and classroom practice.
proposals.novelty: While belief change is a known concern, this study's novelty lies in its longitudinal, multi-method design that directly links quantified belief shifts with rich qualitative data and *observed* mentor teacher practices, moving beyond self-report to triangulate the specific influences within the field experience context.

proposals.title: The Invisible Curriculum: How Music Teacher Educators' Beliefs About Student Beliefs Shape Program Design
proposals.motivation: Research on music teacher education has focused on student beliefs and on effective professional development, but the link between these areas remains underexplored. Understanding how teacher educators' perceptions of their students' beliefs (e.g., about teaching, musical ability, or equity) influence curricular and pedagogical decisions is critical. This 'inverse problem' addresses a core, yet implicit, driver of program quality and innovation in pre-service training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that music teacher educators' design choices (e.g., course sequencing, clinical experiences, assessment methods) are systematically influenced by their often-unexamined beliefs about the stability or malleability of their students' pre-existing beliefs. Specifically, educators who view student beliefs as more malleable will design more experiential and reflective curricula.
proposals.method: This qualitative multi-case study will employ purposeful sampling of 8-10 music teacher educators from diverse institutions. Data collection will involve: (1) semi-structured interviews using a belief elicitation protocol adapted from Richardson (1996) and Pajares (1992); (2) artifact analysis of syllabi, program maps, and assignment prompts; and (3) stimulated recall interviews where educators explain the design rationale behind specific artifacts. Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis and constant comparative methods to identify patterns linking expressed beliefs to documented design features.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced model of the belief-to-design pipeline in music teacher education, moving beyond descriptions of 'best practices' to explain their origins. It will offer the field a reflective framework for educator self-assessment and program evaluation, directly addressing calls to 'rethink' music teacher learning by examining the designers themselves.
proposals.novelty: While studies examine teacher beliefs and curriculum separately, this project uniquely investigates the causal link *from* educator belief *to* program structure, specifically focusing on beliefs *about student beliefs*. This 'second-order' focus on the educator's mental model of the student is a novel lens for understanding curriculum design.

proposals.title: Shifting the Locus of Validation: A Self-Study of Practice (S-STEP) Designed to Measure K-12 Student Musical Growth as the Primary Outcome
proposals.motivation: Self-study of teacher education practices (S-STEP) has been a vital methodology for improving teacher educator reflexivity and program design. However, its focus has predominantly been on educator understanding and professional growth, with student learning outcomes often treated as secondary or implied. In the pre-2026 context of increased accountability in education and calls for evidence-based practice, there is a critical need to demonstrate how reflective teacher education practices directly and measurably impact K-12 student learning. This study addresses a gap by rigorously linking the S-STEP process to tangible student development.
proposals.hypothesis: That a purposefully designed S-STEP inquiry, where the research questions, data collection, and validation mechanisms are explicitly structured around measuring pre/post-change in K-12 student competencies, will produce more compelling, externally valid evidence of music teacher education effectiveness than S-STEP studies focused primarily on the educator's self-understanding.
proposals.method: The researcher (a music teacher educator) will implement a modified instructional unit in a secondary music methods course, explicitly targeting a specific, measurable student skill (e.g., compositional creativity assessed via the Consensual Assessment Technique, or rhythmic accuracy via digital audio analysis). The S-STEP cycle will involve planning the intervention, teaching it, and then collecting multi-source data: (1) pre/post artifacts of K-12 students' musical work from the teacher candidates' field placements, (2) reflective journals from the teacher candidates on their instructional decisions, and (3) the researcher's own reflective memos. Data analysis will triangulate quantitative change in student artifacts with qualitative thematic analysis of candidate and researcher reflections to explain the mechanisms behind the measured student outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a concrete methodological template for conducting outcome-oriented S-STEP research, strengthening the argument for the direct impact of reflective teacher education. It will contribute a validated case study linking specific music teacher education pedagogies to demonstrable gains in P-12 student learning, offering a model for accountability within the arts. The findings will be published in the Journal of Music Teacher Education to guide future S-STEP design.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP values improved student learning, this study innovates by making measurable student change the primary, non-negotiable validation standard for the inquiry's success, fundamentally re-centering the methodology's endpoint from educator cognition to student competency. It operationalizes this shift with a mixed-methods design uncommon in traditional, qualitatively dominant S-STEP work.

proposals.title: Beyond the Interview: A Self-Study of Teacher Practice Using Student Artifacts as Primary Data in Music Teacher Education
proposals.motivation: In music teacher education, understanding student learning often relies on self-reported data from interviews or surveys, which can be influenced by recall bias and social desirability. This reliance may obscure a direct view of the learning process as it unfolds in authentic educational tasks. Grounded in pre-2026 calls to move beyond traditional professional development models, this study addresses the need for methods that capture the tangible, often tacit, evidence of student thinking and skill development.
proposals.hypothesis: Analyzing authentic student work products (e.g., lesson plans, video teaching excerpts, reflective journals, curriculum projects) as the sole data source in a self-study will yield distinct, more concrete, and instructionally actionable insights into preservice teacher learning compared to insights primarily derived from interview data.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative Self-Study of Teacher Education Practices (S-STEP) methodology. The researcher-instructor will collect and analyze a curated portfolio of work products from a single cohort in a secondary music methods course. Analysis will involve a multi-phase thematic analysis: first, a holistic review of artifacts; second, a structured coding process using a priori codes from course objectives and emergent codes from the data; and third, a constant comparative method to identify patterns of development, misconceptions, and evidence of pedagogical reasoning. No interviews or surveys will be conducted; all claims will be grounded solely in the artifact data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a methodological model for music teacher educators to leverage student artifacts as a primary, rich data source for improving their own practice. It will contribute empirical findings on the specific competencies and challenges evident in preservice teachers' authentic work, informing more responsive and evidence-based course design. The work aligns with the field's shift towards practice-based teacher education.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP is established, explicitly forbidding interview data and relying exclusively on a systematic analysis of student artifacts represents a novel methodological constraint within music teacher education research. This approach prioritizes the material traces of learning over retrospective accounts, offering a different lens on student development.

proposals.title: Charting the Arc of Transformation: A Longitudinal Self-Study of a Music Teacher Educator's Practice and Identity Development
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, professional development for music teachers, including teacher educators, is often critiqued as episodic and fragmented, failing to capture the nuanced, long-term process of pedagogical growth. While S-STEP (Self-Study of Teacher Education Practices) is a established methodology for reflective inquiry, its application in music teacher education has typically been limited to single courses or academic terms, providing only snapshots of development. A longitudinal design is necessary to understand how educator identity, curricular decisions, and theoretical frameworks evolve in response to iterative cycles of teaching, reflection, and student feedback over years.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-year, longitudinal S-STEP study will reveal non-linear, context-dependent pathways of pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) development in music teacher education, demonstrating that significant shifts in educator identity and practice are precipitated by critical incidents and sustained reflective dialogue that are only visible across an extended timeframe.
proposals.method: This study employs a longitudinal S-STEP design spanning three consecutive academic years. The primary researcher will maintain a structured reflective journal, documenting planning, in-the-moment decisions, and post-teaching analyses for a core music teacher education course. Data sources will include annual video recordings of key class sessions, anonymized student work and mid/end-term feedback, and memos from critical friend dialogues held bi-annually with a trusted peer. Analysis will follow a multi-phase qualitative process: first, within-year thematic coding of journals and feedback; second, cross-year constant comparative analysis to identify evolving themes, pivotal turning points, and the consolidation of pedagogical principles.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rich, empirical model for conducting longitudinal self-study in music education, moving the field beyond point-in-time reflections. It will generate a nuanced theory of long-term music teacher educator development, identifying key catalysts and patterns of growth. The findings will offer concrete strategies for structuring sustained professional learning for both pre-service and in-service music educators.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP is known in music education, applying it systematically across multiple years to trace the arc of an educator's development is novel. This approach uniquely captures the consolidation of professional identity and the long-term impact of curricular innovations, which are invisible in shorter-term studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Self-Study of Teacher Education Practices (S-STEP) on Preservice Music Teacher Development: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Performance and Retention
proposals.motivation: Self-Study of Teacher Education Practices (S-STEP) is a vital, well-established qualitative methodology for improving music teacher educator pedagogy. However, its impact has been measured primarily through reflective narratives and educator self-reports, leaving a gap in empirical evidence linking S-STEP interventions to concrete, measurable outcomes for preservice teachers. In the pre-2026 context of calls for accountability and evidence-based practice in teacher education, this lack of quantitative data limits the broader adoption and systematic refinement of S-STEP approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: That music teacher education courses incorporating structured S-STEP methodologies will yield quantitatively measurable improvements in preservice teachers' observed teaching performance and higher rates of program retention and entry into the teaching profession, compared to cohorts from courses using traditional, non-S-STEP pedagogies.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal design. Quantitative data will be collected from two cohorts of preservice music teachers over three years. Primary measures include: 1) Pre/post scores on a validated teaching performance rubric (e.g., edTPA or a domain-specific music teaching observation protocol) analyzed using repeated-measures ANOVA; 2) Retention and career-entry rates tracked via institutional records and follow-up surveys, analyzed with chi-square tests and logistic regression to control for covariates. These quantitative measures will be explicitly triangulated with existing qualitative S-STEP data (e.g., instructor journals, student reflections) to provide a comprehensive analysis of impact.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence base for the efficacy of S-STEP in music teacher education, moving the field beyond anecdotal justification. It will offer a replicable model for integrating outcome measures into self-study research, strengthening its validity and practical utility. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective, evidence-driven professional learning for both preservice and inservice music educators.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP is mature, its integration with rigorous, longitudinal quantitative outcome measures targeting student-teacher performance and retention is novel. This approach moves the methodology from a focus on educator self-understanding to a direct, empirical test of its downstream effects on teacher candidates, a non-obvious synthesis of reflective practice and outcome-based assessment.

proposals.title: Illuminating the Pedagogy of Teacher Education: A Cross-Institutional Self-Study of Practice (S-STEP) Collaboration in Music Teacher Preparation
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, professional development for music teacher educators is often siloed within individual institutions, limiting opportunities for critical reflection on shared pedagogical challenges. The S-STEP methodology is well-established for individual educator inquiry, but its potential for collaborative, comparative analysis across different program contexts remains underexplored. This gap hinders the development of a robust, shared knowledge base about the complex act of teaching future teachers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that forming a structured, cross-institutional S-STEP research team will reveal both common, foundational challenges in music teacher education pedagogy and context-specific manifestations of those challenges, leading to more nuanced and transferable principles of practice than single-instructor studies can provide.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will form a collaborative S-STEP team of 4-6 music teacher educators from diverse university settings (e.g., research-intensive, liberal arts, varying geographic regions). Using a structured, multi-cycle approach, each educator will first conduct an individual self-study (e.g., analyzing video of their own teaching, student work, and reflective journals) focused on a shared thematic problem (e.g., facilitating critical reflection in methods courses). The team will then engage in iterative, comparative analysis through regular virtual meetings, using cross-case analysis techniques to identify patterns, divergences, and underlying assumptions across the individual datasets, thereby constructing a collective interpretation.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a validated model for collaborative S-STEP research that moves beyond the individual case. It will generate a rich, comparative description of music teacher educator pedagogy, identifying both universal tensions and context-dependent strategies. The findings will provide a framework for other teacher educators to engage in similar cross-institutional professional learning communities.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP is common, applying it through a deliberately designed, synchronous cross-institutional collaboration for comparative analysis is a novel methodological advancement in music teacher education research. It shifts the focus from the individual 'self' to a collective 'selves-in-context,' offering a new pathway for building the field's professional knowledge.

proposals.title: Design-Based Research for Adaptive Pedagogy: Iterative Development of a Secondary General Music Curriculum Module on Digital Composition
proposals.motivation: Traditional, one-off professional development in music education often fails to create sustained, context-sensitive changes in teacher practice and student learning. Pre-2026 literature in STEM education demonstrates that Design-Based Research (DBR) effectively bridges theory and practice by co-developing interventions with educators in authentic settings. Applying this rigorous, iterative methodology to music education addresses a critical gap in how we design and implement curriculum that is responsive to diverse classroom needs.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing DBR cycles, where music education researchers and secondary school music teachers collaboratively design, enact, and refine a curriculum module on digital composition, will yield a more pedagogically robust, adaptable, and effective instructional framework than one developed through traditional, linear design processes.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-year DBR framework with three iterative cycles in partnership with three secondary school music teachers. Each cycle consists of four phases: 1) Analysis of practical problems via teacher interviews and classroom observation; 2) Co-design of the curriculum module (e.g., lesson sequences, project-based learning tasks for digital audio workstations); 3) Enactment and study of the module in classrooms, collecting data from student work, teacher reflective journals, and video recordings; 4) Systematic reflection and redesign based on qualitative analysis (thematic coding) and quantitative analysis (pre/post assessments of student compositional self-efficacy). The refined design principles from one cycle inform the next.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a theoretically grounded, empirically tested, and practically viable curriculum module for digital music composition. It will contribute a detailed case study and a set of design principles for music-specific DBR, offering the field a replicable model for developing contextually responsive curriculum and transforming how music teacher professional learning is conceptualized and studied.
proposals.novelty: While DBR is established in STEM, its systematic application to core content and pedagogical design in music education is novel. This project moves beyond evaluating existing programs to actively generating new, adaptable curriculum knowledge through partnership, directly addressing calls to 'rethink' music teacher learning.

proposals.title: Capturing the Dynamics of Affective Experience: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Music Student Teachers and Their Pupils During Fieldwork
proposals.motivation: Traditional professional development and teacher education research often relies on retrospective self-reports, which are subject to memory bias and fail to capture the real-time, contextualized emotional and cognitive states that shape teaching and learning. In music education, where fieldwork is a high-stakes, interactive, and affectively charged component of teacher preparation, understanding these in-the-moment dynamics is critical for designing effective, personalized support. Grounded in pre-2026 calls to move 'beyond traditional professional development,' this study addresses a significant gap in understanding the lived, moment-to-moment reality of the music classroom.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the affective states of music student teachers and their K-12 pupils during fieldwork are highly variable and co-regulated, with specific teaching tasks (e.g., rehearsal, individual instruction, classroom management) and contextual factors (e.g., lesson phase, group size) predicting distinct patterns of positive and negative affect for both groups.
proposals.method: This study will employ the Experience Sampling Method (ESM) using a dedicated mobile application (e.g., mEMA or a custom app built using a platform like MetricWire) to collect ecological momentary assessments. A cohort of music student teachers (n=20-25) and a sample of their pupils (n=~200) will be signaled at semi-random intervals 3-5 times per lesson over a 4-week fieldwork placement. Each prompt will deliver a brief survey measuring affect (using the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule - Short Form, PANAS-SF) and contextual variables (e.g., activity type, perceived success). Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling (MLM) to account for nested data (moments within individuals within classrooms), examining within-person variability and cross-level interactions between teacher and pupil affect and contextual predictors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first fine-grained, ecologically valid map of affective dynamics in music teacher fieldwork, moving beyond static snapshots. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for studying teacher learning in situ and yield practical insights for mentoring and structuring field experiences to optimize emotional and pedagogical outcomes for both novice teachers and their students.
proposals.novelty: While ESM is used in general education and psychology, its application to music teacher education, and particularly the simultaneous sampling of linked teacher-student dyads/triads in a real-world performance-based setting, is novel. This approach directly operationalizes the call to rethink teacher learning by capturing the 'in-the-moment' processes that traditional professional development models overlook.

proposals.title: Micro-Reflection in Practice: A Self-Study of a Single Lesson in Music Teacher Education
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature on music teacher professional development critiques traditional, episodic workshops as insufficient for fostering deep, contextualized learning. There is a recognized need for models that integrate reflection directly into teaching practice. Self-study of teacher education practices (S-STEP) offers a powerful framework for such inquiry, but its application is typically broad, examining courses or programs over time, potentially overlooking the granular, moment-to-moment decisions that constitute pedagogical artistry.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the S-STEP reflective cycle (planning, acting, observing, reflecting, re-planning) with intense focus on a single class session will yield unique, high-resolution insights into the instructor's pedagogical decision-making and student engagement that are often obscured in longer-term self-studies.
proposals.method: The researcher will design and deliver one specific 75-minute session in a music teacher education course (e.g., on facilitating peer feedback in rehearsals). The S-STEP cycle will be applied meticulously: 1) Documenting the planning rationale and anticipated challenges; 2) Teaching the lesson while collecting multi-modal data (video recording, student work artifacts, researcher's in-the-moment notes); 3) Conducting a systematic analysis of this data, focusing on critical incidents and student responses; 4) Engaging a critical friend to review the data and analysis, challenging assumptions. The analysis will use qualitative coding pre-2026 methods (e.g., thematic analysis, constant comparison) to identify emergent themes about pedagogical reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a detailed, replicable model for micro-level reflective practice, providing music teacher educators with a structured approach to examine and improve their own teaching in real-time. It will generate rich, contextual knowledge about the relationship between specific instructional choices and immediate student learning within a single educational encounter.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP is established, its deliberate application to the scale of a single lesson is novel, shifting the focus from programmatic reflection to an intensive, forensic examination of a contained pedagogical event, thereby making the tacit knowledge of expert teaching more visible and accessible.

proposals.title: Flipping the Lens: A Collaborative Action Research Model for Student-Led Inquiry into Music Teacher Educator Practice
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of professional development and teacher education often position educators as the sole experts and students as passive recipients. This unidirectional flow of feedback limits opportunities for educators to critically reflect on their own teaching practices based on structured student input. Grounded in pre-2026 calls to rethink inservice learning and leverage technology for connection in music teacher education, this study addresses a gap in empowering pre-service teachers as co-inquirers into pedagogical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, student-led collaborative inquiry cycle into a music teacher education course will: 1) enhance teacher educator reflexivity and adaptive practice, and 2) develop pre-service teachers' metacognitive and observational skills relevant to their future careers.
proposals.method: This design-based research study will be conducted within an undergraduate music methods course. Students will form small 'inquiry teams' tasked with observing and collecting data on specific educator practices (e.g., questioning techniques, feedback delivery, use of course videos). Using a simplified qualitative coding framework (e.g., thematic analysis of their own reflections, frequency counts of practice types), teams will analyze patterns and collaboratively prepare formative feedback reports. The educator will then integrate this feedback in an iterative cycle, with teams tracking changes. Data sources include student inquiry reports, reflective journals, educator memos, and course artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a concrete, replicable model for 'inverting' the observational focus in teacher education, aligning with contemporary themes of collaborative professionalism. It will provide empirical evidence on the impacts of such inversion on both educator development and student learning, offering a new pathway for developing reflective practice in both groups.
proposals.novelty: While student evaluations are common, this proposal systematizes student inquiry as a formal, collaborative, and formative research process within the course structure, moving beyond summative feedback to position students as partners in pedagogical investigation.

proposals.title: The Researcher as Instrument: A Meta-Study of Transformative Learning in S-STEP Music Teacher Education Research
proposals.motivation: Self-study of teacher education practices (S-STEP) is a well-established methodology for improving teaching, but its impact on the *researchers themselves* remains under-examined as a formal object of study. This gap is significant because S-STEP's core tenet is that the researcher's own transformation is integral to improving practice. Understanding this impact is crucial for validating S-STEP's methodological rigor and for designing more effective researcher education and mentorship within music teacher education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic analysis of S-STEP researchers' reflective data will reveal identifiable, transformative learning pathways characterized by specific cognitive and affective shifts. Furthermore, we hypothesize that these pathways are catalyzed by distinct methodological components of the S-STEP process.
proposals.method: This qualitative meta-study will employ a two-phase design. First, a purposive sample of 8-10 published S-STEP studies in music teacher education (from journals like JTME, 2015-2025) will be analyzed using directed content analysis to extract researchers' reported personal and professional changes. Second, the lead researcher will conduct a concurrent, reflexive self-study of her own S-STEP process while mentoring a doctoral student through a new S-STEP project. Data from both phases (published texts, reflective journals, dialogue transcripts) will be triangulated and analyzed using thematic analysis to construct a model of researcher transformation.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a validated framework for understanding and assessing the developmental impact of S-STEP on music teacher educators. It will provide empirical evidence for the personal-professional growth claims inherent to the methodology, strengthening its scholarly standing. The findings will offer concrete guidance for structuring S-STEP inquiry to maximize transformative learning for both novice and experienced researchers.
proposals.novelty: While S-STEP literature often mentions researcher growth anecdotally, this study systematically makes the *researcher's learning process* the primary object of empirical investigation, applying the methodology reflexively to itself. It moves beyond evaluating S-STEP's outcomes for teaching practice to analyze its intrinsic function as a professional learning mechanism.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing Music Teacher Education for Lifelong Learning: A Design-Based Study of University Programs Integrating Older Adult Learners
proposals.motivation: Demographic shifts and the lifelong learning movement challenge higher education's traditional focus on young adults. Pre-2026, programs like Elderhostel (now Road Scholar) demonstrated robust demand for non-credit educational experiences among older adults, yet university music teacher education programs largely treat this demographic as peripheral. This creates a missed opportunity for music educators to develop competencies in teaching across the lifespan and for universities to engage a growing, educationally active population.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating older adult learners as a core demographic in the design and delivery of university music teacher education courses will positively impact pre-service music teachers' conceptions of learner diversity, pedagogical adaptability, and professional identity as facilitators of community music engagement.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative design-based research (DBR) methodology within a university music teacher education program. Researchers will collaborate with faculty to redesign an existing undergraduate course (e.g., 'General Music Methods') to include a co-learning module where pre-service teachers and community-dwelling older adults (recruited via partnerships with senior centers) engage in collaborative music-making and lesson planning. Data collection will include: 1) pre/post semi-structured interviews with pre-service teachers focusing on beliefs about age and learning; 2) video analysis of teaching episodes and collaborative sessions; 3) reflective journals from pre-service teachers; and 4) focus groups with older adult participants. Thematic analysis will be used to identify shifts in teacher understanding and program design principles.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a novel framework for 'lifespan-inclusive' music teacher education, moving beyond older adults as a specialty topic. It will produce empirical evidence on how intergenerational learning contexts influence teacher development and offer a concrete, transferable model for university program redesign that addresses broader institutional goals for community engagement and enrollment diversification.
proposals.novelty: While research exists on community music for older adults and standalone professional development for teachers, this study uniquely positions the systemic integration of older learners as a catalyst for transforming core *music teacher education* curriculum and pedagogy, not as an add-on elective or outreach activity.

proposals.title: From Grassroots to Governance: A Longitudinal Study of the '20%+ by 2020' Initiative's Impact on Leadership Culture in Music Education
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, initiatives like '20%+ by 2020' emerged as grassroots efforts to address gender disparities in leadership, mirroring broader societal calls for equity. In music education, while female participation is high at the student and early-career levels, significant underrepresentation persists in leadership roles (e.g., department chairs, conference heads, journal editors). Understanding the efficacy of such campaigns within our field is critical for moving beyond symbolic gestures toward sustainable structural change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the '20%+ by 2020' initiative, as a form of grassroots professional mobilization, has had a measurable positive impact on the representation of women in formal music education leadership positions and, more importantly, has shifted the informal professional culture to be more inclusive of diverse leadership pathways.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will employ a longitudinal, sequential explanatory design. First, quantitative data on female representation in key leadership roles (e.g., state MEA presidents, NASM council chairs, editorial boards) from 2015-2025 will be collected and analyzed for trend changes. Second, qualitative data will be gathered via semi-structured interviews and focus groups with 30-40 educators (both those who participated in the campaign and those who did not) to explore perceptions of cultural change, mentorship networks, and barriers. Analysis will involve descriptive/inferential statistics and thematic analysis using a constant comparative method.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical analysis of a specific equity campaign's long-term impact within music teacher education. It will contribute a model for evaluating grassroots professional development aimed at systemic change and offer evidence-based recommendations for future initiatives targeting diversity in educational leadership.
